topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s crisp papers felt like sandpaper against my palms. I stared at the deed, the ink blurring under the harsh afternoon light streaming through the dusty window. My jaw was clenched so tight I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. "This is ridiculous," I muttered, the sound a low, ragged hiss in the silence of the solicitor's office. My brother, Mark, was already sketching blueprints on a notepad, a picture of placid acceptance. I slammed the folder onto the mahogany desk, the sudden noise making the nearby receptionist jump. "We're not doing this," I stated, my voice dangerously level, though the tremor in my hands betrayed the storm brewing inside me. I wanted to rip the papers in half, to see the mess they would make.

[story 2]

The scent of mothballs and aged velvet clung to the air of Grandma’s old manor, a suffocating blanket. I paced the overgrown garden, my boots crunching loudly on the dead leaves. Every shadow seemed to stretch and distort, mocking my sense of entitlement. "She left this to us?" I spat the words out, kicking a loose stone across the flagstones with unnecessary force. My chest felt tight, constricted, as if someone had tied a vise around my ribs. Mark was upstairs, probably already planning how to monetize the inheritance, and his cheerful optimism grated on my nerves like grit in my mouth. I stopped abruptly by the grand, peeling front door, my knuckles white as I gripped the brass handle, intending to force it open.

[story 3]

I watched Mark carefully arrange the antique porcelain on the mantelpiece, his movements too smooth, too composed. A hot, prickly sensation rose up my neck, making the hairs stand on end. I didn't look at him, focusing instead on the cracked portrait above the fireplace, the eyes of the long-dead matriarch seeming to judge my every move. "This whole situation is a joke," I announced, my voice sharp enough to cut glass. I turned away, walking toward the drawing-room, my steps heavy and deliberate. I wanted to shatter something—the fragile peace, the expected harmony—anything to release the pressure building behind my eyes. My breath hitched, shallow and fast, as I surveyed the imposing, silent rooms, feeling utterly out of place and profoundly unsettled.
